coredata.dc:title: The construction of a FRET assembly by using gold nanoclusters and carbon dots and their application as a ratiometric probe for cysteine detection
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Xu
Zhang, Chun-Xia
Zhang, Lening
Xue, Ya-Rong
Li, Hong-Wei
Wu, Yuqing
coredata.dc:description: Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a powerful tool to study single molecular conformation and/or distance changes between two fluorophores, which is mainly depended on the combinations and spatial distance between the donor and acceptor. The present study has built a FRET assembly by using gold nanoclusters capped by glutathione (AuNCs@GSH) and carbon dots (CDs) prepared by polyethyleneimine (PEI) through supramolecular interactions. The assembly is characterized in detail by employing Zeta potential, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra, the luminescent decay curves and TEM images, which indicate the AuNCs are adhered to the surface of CDs. In addition, the transfer efficiency between them has been calculated to be 65.7%, which is sufficient enough for practical applications, and particularly, the quantum yield (QY) of AuNCs@GSH is increased from 3.07% to 5.35% via the assembly. Moreover, the FRET assembly is used as a ratiometric probe for cysteine detection in showing a limit of detection (LOD) at 100 nM, a very high selectivity to cysteine among amino acids as well as a strong anti-interference on them, especially, it was feasible for cysteine detection in fetal calf serum, which showed very high potential in the diagnose of cysteine-related disease.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoclusters
Carbon dots
Assembly
FRET
Cysteine detection

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The preparation of GSH protected gold nanoclusters (AuNCs@GSH)
The assembly of CDs and AuNCs@GSH
The application of FRET assembly as a ratiometric probe for cysteine detection
Fabrication and characterization of AuNCs@GSH and CDs
The assembly of AuNCs@GSH and CDs
The detection of cysteine by using CDs-AuNCs assembly
Response mechanism of CDs-AuNCs assembly to cysteine
The detection of cysteine in fetal calf serum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the processes to prepare and assembly of AuNCs@GSH and CDs together.

(A) The photo-emission (red solid lines) and -excitation (dashed line) spectra of AuNCs@GSH; (B) Uv–vis absorption spectrum of AuNCs@GSH; (C) Typical TEM images (Inset: crystal lattice of a representative) and (D) statistic size distributions of AuNCs@GSH. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) The photo-emission (blue solid lines) and photo-excitation (dashed line) spectra and (B) Uv–vis absorption spectrum of CDs; (C) Typical TEM image (Inset: crystal lattice) and (D) statistic size distributions of CDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) The fluorescence spectra of 250 μg/mL CDs in water being titrated with different amount of AuNCs@GSH (0–600 μg/mL); (B) the related ratio changes of fluorescence intensity at 605–465 nm (λex = 345 nm); The corresponding photographes of CDs (No. 1) in the presence of different amount of AuNCs@GSH (No. 2–11, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 500, 600 μg/mL) under UV light (C) and sunlight (D).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs-AuNCs (50 and 100 μg/mL) in HEPES-NaOH buffer solution (pH = 7.4) which were measured 60 min before and after adding different amounts of cysteine (Cys, from 1.0 to 200 μM); B) The plot of the fluorescence intensities ratio in changing with the concentration of cysteine (λex = 345 nm).

(A) The comparison of fluorescence spectra of CDs-AuNCs (50 and 100 μg/mL) in the presence of different kind of amino acids (100 μM) in HEPES-NaOH buffer solution (pH = 7.4); (B) The corresponding intensity ratio at 605 and 465 nm changes. From left to right is for Blank, Glycine, Alanine, Valine, Leucine, Isoleucine, Phenylalanine, Tryptophan, Tyrosine, Aspartic acid, Histidine, Asparagine, Glutamic acid, Lysine, Glutamine, Methionine, Arginine, Serine, Threonine, Cysteine, and Proline, being abbreviated as Blank, Gly, Ala, Val, Leu, Ile, Phe, Trp, Tyr, Asp, His, Asn, Glu, Lys, Gln, Met, Arg, Ser, Thr, Cys and Pro, respectively.

head.ce:title: The construction of a FRET assembly by using gold nanoclusters and carbon dots and their application as a ratiometric probe for cysteine detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Institute of Theoretical Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Thoracic Surgery, China-Japan Union Hospital, Jilin University, Changchun 130033, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a powerful tool to study single molecular conformation and/or distance changes between two fluorophores, which is mainly depended on the combinations and spatial distance between the donor and acceptor. The present study has built a FRET assembly by using gold nanoclusters capped by glutathione (AuNCs@GSH) and carbon dots (CDs) prepared by polyethyleneimine (PEI) through supramolecular interactions. The assembly is characterized in detail by employing Zeta potential, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra, the luminescent decay curves and TEM images, which indicate the AuNCs are adhered to the surface of CDs. In addition, the transfer efficiency between them has been calculated to be 65.7%, which is sufficient enough for practical applications, and particularly, the quantum yield (QY) of AuNCs@GSH is increased from 3.07% to 5.35% via the assembly. Moreover, the FRET assembly is used as a ratiometric probe for cysteine detection in showing a limit of detection (LOD) at 100 nM, a very high selectivity to cysteine among amino acids as well as a strong anti-interference on them, especially, it was feasible for cysteine detection in fetal calf serum, which showed very high potential in the diagnose of cysteine-related disease.

ce:section.ce:para: Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a unique technique for probing subtle changes in molecular conformation, association, and separation between donor and acceptor in the range of 1–10 nm [1–3]. It has been widely applied to the biological investigations, especially, to exploring the spatial relationships between two labeled sites in structures and/or supramolecular assembly [4,5]. The intrinsic mechanism of FRET is that the donor fluorophore is excited by absorbing a photon, and then the energy is transferred to a nearby acceptor molecule through a non-radiation pathway [6,7]. Up to now, large amount of fluorescence materials have been applied to build FRET, for example, organic dyes, fluorescent proteins, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and so on [8–10]. For that, various strategies as covalent couple [11], electrostatic attachment [12], and specific supramolecular interaction have been employed [13–15]. Though significant progresses have been obtained in the field, there are still important issues to be resolved. To our knowledge to construct useful FRET systems, favorable photophysical properties are pre-requisite, including (1) well-overlapped emission of donor and absorption of acceptor, (2) donors and acceptors with strong brightness, and (3) near-perfect efficiency in their energy transfer [16]. Therefore, expanding of the fluorophore library and exploring new donor/acceptor combinations will ultimately promote FRET pairs and particularly the scope of practical applications [1,9,16,17], such as improving the cellular permeability, resisting degradation, against photobleaching and reducing cytotoxicity.

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted more and more interests due to their excellent properties as small size (2–10 nm), nontoxicity, biocompatibility and feasible cell permeability, which have been extensively applied to analytical detection and imaging [18–20]. The most extending characteristic and significant property of CDs is the relatively strong fluorescence emission, which can be easily controlled through tunable band gap via element doping and surface functionalization [21,22]. However, the blue-green emission (400–500 nm) of CDs limited their biological application as the strong overlapping with cellular auto-fluorescence. Recently, fluorescent gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) exhibit strong luminescence in a wider optical range (from blue to near-infrared) and show potential applications in bio-labelling and imaging [23–27]. However, the low fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of AuNCs should be improved largely. Therefore, the combined use of CDs and AuNCs together might suggest a new possibility to perform perfect fluorescence materials although the related study is rare. Ju et al. have constructed a binary heterogeneous nano-complex through covalent binding of AuNCs and CDs, which show dual-emission property without FRET effect [28]. Niu et al. have obtained a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on a covalent-binding of CDs and AuNCs but only showed a slight FRET [29]. Li et al. have built a ratiometric fluorescence probe with branch poly(ethylenimine)-capped CDs and dihydrolipoicacid-protected AuNCs through a covalent coupling, where the FRET efficiency is still low either (only about 40%) [30].

In the present study, we have designed a FRET assembly through a non-covalent interaction, where the define carbon dots (CDs) and AuNCs are performed as perfect donor and acceptor. In comparison to the covalent strategy, the supramolecular assembly is controllable, reversible and well-organized, which could be used to regulate the fluorescence performance of FRET system more easily. In addition, the driving force is mainly attributed to the electrostatic interactions between them, which is highly affected and regulated by pH. Especially, the new FRET assembly can be successfully applied to detect cysteine as a ratiometric probe, which shows high selectivity, sensitivity and strong anti-interference. Therefore, it will supply a convenient assay for the diagnose of cysteine-related disease including heart disease, sepsis, HIV and so on, and will show great applications in biotechnology and life science.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI, Mw = 10000), glutathione (GSH, >99.9), 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid (HEPES, >99.9%), l-cysteine (Cys, >99.9%) and other amino acids (AAs) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co. Ltd. (China). And chloroauric acid hydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, >99.9%) was bought from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company (China). All other chemicals were obtained from commercial suppliers and used without further purification. Distilled water (ρ = 18.2 MΩ cm, 25 °C) was obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q purification assembly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of GSH protected gold nanoclusters (AuNCs@GSH)
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of GSH protected gold nanoclusters (AuNCs@GSH) was performed based on a newly developed hydrothermal method [31], which could be summarized as: A GSH solution (0.36–0.60 mL, 100 mM) was mixed with 10 mM of HAuCl4 at molar ratios from 1:1.6 to 1:2.4 for HAuCl4 to GSH at room temperature. Then extra distilled water was added to dilute it to 10 mL and moved to Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After heat at 100, 110 or 120 °C, separately, for 1–3 h, the solution was dialyzed with a 3.5 kDa cut-off bag extensively against doubly distilled water for 2 days by changing solution every 4 h. For quantitative analysis, the product was lyophilized to powder and then dissolved in water to get a stock solution with a concentration of 5.0 mg/mL. The prepared AuNCs@GSH were extremely stable as which can be stored at 4 °C for 2 months without fluorescence change.

The synthesis of carbon dots followed a previous reported method by Zhu et al. [32] with minor modifications, which could be summarized as follows: 500 μL PEI (20 mM) was diluted with 9.5 mL ultrapure water and then moved to Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After being heated at 160 °C for 10 h, the autoclave was removed out and naturally cooled down to the room temperature. Finally, the product was dialyzed with a 10 kDa cut-off bag extensively against distilled water for more than 48 h with solution changing every 8 h to remove all impurity. Finally, the product was lyophilized to obtain CDs in powder.

The fluorescence spectra were obtained by using a Shimadzu (Japan) RF-5301PC spectrophotometer. To get more stable results, the lamp was kept on for 0.5 h before measurement. The UV–vis absorption spectra were measured by a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected with JEM-2200FS (Jeol Ltd., Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The absolute quantum yields (QY) of the samples were conducted on fluorescence spectrophotometer with an integrating sphere (Edinburgh, FLS-920). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were carried out on an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with a monochromic X-ray source (Al Kα line, 1486.6 eV) and the charging shift was corrected by the binding energy of C1s at 284.6 eV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The assembly of CDs and AuNCs@GSH
ce:section.ce:para: All spectroscopic measurements of CDs and AuNCs@GSH were performed in HEPES-NaOH (10.0 mM, pH = 7.4) buffer solution, and the concentration of CDs in the fluorescent experiments was kept at 250, 50, or 5.0 μg/mL for different purpose. To optimize the assembly of CDs and AuNCs@GSH, the concentration of CDs was kept at 250 μg/mL and titrated with different amount of AuNCs@GSH, which was monitored by fluorescence spectrophotometer. For the pH titration, the measurement was conducted in a solution containing 0.1 M NaNO3 to keep the ionic strength. All measurements were performed in HEPES-NaOH (10.0 mM, pH = 7.4) buffer solution with 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cuvettes (4 mL volume), and the samples were stock-still for 60 min prior to the experiment. And an excitation wavelength at 345 nm was used throughout the experiments except where indicated. Then the energy transfer procedure was monitored by using the time-resolved fluorescence spectra, which was recorded by using a time-correlated single photon counting technique, equipped with an Edinburgh Analytical Instruments-FLS920. The process was conducted by fixing the amount of CDs (250 μg/mL) and titrated with different amount of AuNCs@GSH (100, 200, 300 μg/mL) in HEPES-NaOH buffer solution (10.0 mM, pH = 7.4), and the excitation wavelength was fixed at 365 nm and the intensity at 460 nm was collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of FRET assembly as a ratiometric probe for cysteine detection
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized supramolecular assembly was dissolved in 10.0 mM HEPES-NaOH buffer solution (pH = 7.4), then being titrated with different amount of cysteine and monitored by fluorescence spectrophotometer. The assembly solution was also titrated with different types of other amino acids (AAs) in the respective stock solution (100 mM). To obtain the limit of detection the assembly solution was diluted with 9-fold HEPES-NaOH buffer solution, and titrated with 0.25–20 μM cysteine. The stock solutions (100 mM) were obtained through dissolving the AAs with distilled water directly. And all the fluorescence detections were conducted in the HEPES-NaOH buffer solution (10.0 mM, pH = 7.4) with a fixed excitation wavelength at 345 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of AuNCs@GSH and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The AuNCs@GSH were prepared by using a one-pot hydrothermal method, being depicted in detail in part 2. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the product showed a red, broad emission peak centered at 605 nm (λex = 420 nm), which was consistent well with the literature reports [30]. Meanwhile, the absorption spectrum showed a typical broad absorption at ∼420 nm but no absorbance peak between 500 and 600 nm existed (Fig. 1B), demonstrating no big particles producing in this process. Such result was highly consistent with the previously reported AuNCs@GSH containing strong fluorescence, where the sizes were observed to be smaller than 2 nm [33–35]. The temperature, reaction time and the ratio of raw reactants was optimized respectively through monitoring the emission intensity of the produced AuNCs, which demonstrated that 1: 2 of HAuCl4 to GSH and heating at 110 °C for 1.5 h were the best condition to prepare AuNCs (Fig. S1). Therefore, in comparison to the reported methods the newly developed one was quite fast to be completed within 1.5 h, being environment-friendly without extra reducing agent, one-pot, easy operation; particularly, the products were very stable as the fluorescence emission could be kept constant at 4 °C for 2 months.

The prepared AuNCs@GSH were then characterized by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), where both the size and morphology of the Au cores were exhibited clearly. The images revealed that the AuNCs were highly crystalized and mono-dispersive (Fig. 1C), and clear lattice fringes were observed with an interspacing of 0.235 nm (inset in Fig. 1C), corresponding to the d-spacing of the crystal plane of face centered cubic Au (111). Based on the statistics over 200 individual particles in TEM images, the mean core diameter was measured to be 1.35 nm and a large proportion of AuNCs was distributed between 1.00–1.70 nm (Fig. 1D). The wide distribution of the particle size was attributed to the inhomogeneity of the prepared AuNCs@GSH, being consistent with the reported AuNCs [36–38]. Then the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to determine the content and oxidation states of gold in the prepared AuNCs@GSH. As shown in Fig. S2, the AuNCs showed two bands centered at 84.7 and 88.4 eV, respectively, being attributed to the 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 peak of Au [36]. The Au 4f7/2 peak was further deconvoluted into two distinct components (curves in dot) centered at binding energies of 84.0 and 85.0 eV, respectively, being assigned to Au(0) and Au(I). The integrated area of them supplied a proportion of ∼20.5% for Au(0) and ∼79.5% for Au(I) respectively in the particles, indicating the HAuCl4 was reduced extensively in the preparation. Of note, among the particles almost one-fifth gold existed as Au(0), illustrating they were true metal nanoclusters other than the complex of Au(I). That was, Au(0) located in the interior while the Au(I) present at the surface of the particle, and as ligand GSH interacted with Au(I) at the surface to stabilize the AuNCs via the AuS bond.

The blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) were then synthesized by following a reported method [32], which employed teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave as reaction vessel and heat at 160 °C for 10 h. The products showed a strong emission at 465 nm (λex = 345 nm; Fig. 2 A) and a characteristic absorption peak centered at 355 nm (Fig. 2B), being highly consistent with the report [32]. Typical TEM revealed that the CDs were mono-dispersive (Fig. 2C) and the lattice fringes were consistent well with the lattice spacing of 0.210 nm for carbon (inset in Fig. 2C). Based on the statistics over 200 individual particles, the mean core diameter was evaluated to be 4.25 nm and the sizes of CDs were distributed broadly between 1.0–7.0 nm (Fig. 2D), being close to the reported ones [39,40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The assembly of AuNCs@GSH and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Accordingly, an amine and two carboxyl groups were contained for one GSH at the surface of AuNCs@GSH, and at neutral condition negative charges were shown there [41]. In contrast, the CDs synthesized from polyethyleneimine (PEI) retained significant amino groups out of the carbon core, which induced positive charges at the surface of CDs under neutral condition. Thus, it was possible to assemble these two components together via electrostatic interaction because of the opposite charge surfaces between them. To testify this hypothesis, Zeta potential was then test to monitor the surface charge changes during the titration of AuNCs to the solution containing CDs. As shown in Fig. S3, the zeta potential decreased obviously from positive to negative, which confirmed an opposite charge surface and suggested a possible electrostatic binding between AuNCs@GSH and CDs.

Then the titration process was monitored by using fluorescence spectra. The emission intensity of CDs at 465 nm was quenched gradually in the presence of different amount of AuNCs@GSH; meanwhile, that of AuNCs at 605 nm was increased significantly (Fig. 3 A). Therefore, the intensity ratio of them increased significantly in the presence of more AuNCs (Fig. 3B). These results could be explained well with the FRET model, where the fluorescence quenching of donor and the enhancement of acceptor were generally caused. In order to exclude the effect of AuNCs concentrations, a blank test was also performed in buffer solution without CDs. As shown in Fig. S4A, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs increased either with adding of AuNCs to buffer solution, being attributed to the concentration effect. However, the enhanced altitude was much lower than that of the assembly (Fig. S4B), which ensured the FRET phenomenon indeed involved there. That was, the observed fluorescence enhancement composed of two parts, the concentration effect and the FRET effect. As shown in Fig. S4B, the red column attributed to the concentration effect; while of course, the blue column was attributed to the FRET effect. The large heights of blue column indicated the FRET played a vital role in the fluorescence enhancement of AuNCs in the assembly. Therefore, the FRET assembly was constructed successfully, where the define CDs were performed as donor and AuNCs were displayed as acceptor. In comparison to the covalent couple strategy [28–30], the supramolecular assembly method was controllable, reversible and well-organized, particularly, being possible to regulate the fluorescence performance and assembly structure more easily. In addition, the processes exhibited an obvious color changes under the excitation of UV light (360 nm). As shown in Fig. 3C, the blue emission of CDs was gradually changed to orange upon the addition of AuNCs; while the colorless solution of CDs was also gradually transferred to light yellow under sunshine (Fig. 3D). The color changes were attributed to the FRET process directly, which confirmed the assembly of AuNCs@GSH and CDs together successful in further.

Fluorescence lifetime is an intrinsic property that the fluorochrome remains in its excited state prior to returning to its ground state, depending on the nature of the fluorescent sites and the environment [42,43]. Consequently, the fluorescence lifetimes of the FRET assembly were measured by employing time-resolved fluorescence spectra. In general, the fluorescence lifetime of the donor in FRET assembly should be decreased with more acceptor addition [1,44]. As shown in Fig. S5, in the presence of AuNCs@GSH the decay curve at 465 nm for CDs was decreased clearly. A double-exponential fitting on the decay curve revealed the CDs contained two components with lifetime of 3.258 (41.84%) and 6.047 (58.16%) ns, respectively (Table S1), being attributed to two different emissive components of CDs. According to the previous reports, this implies the fluorescence state is due to the carbon center (or conjugated structures) and surface traps [42]. With the addition of more AuNCs@GSH the short lifetime became shorter (0.6123 ns) but the percentage increased dramatically. According to the previous reports about the FRET system [1,44], such results indicated the assembly of CDs and AuNCs induced FRET between them.

The pH effect on the fluorescence emission of the FRET assembly was then investigated. As shown in Fig. S6, the emission of AuNCs@GSH in the assembly of CDs-AuNCs increased continuously at the acidic condition until pH = 5.0, and then decreased quickly with pH increase; while the emission of CDs was kept with a very weak emission until pH = 4.0, and then increased and kept constant until pH = 9.0. After that, it would reach the isoelectric point of the assembly, where the solution became cloudy; and then became clear and clear with pH. When monitoring the emission changes of AuNCs@GSH and CDs with pH, separately, the emission of AuNCs kept constant and increased slowly with a small fluctuation until pH = 10.0; while that of CDs increased at pH = 4.5–5.0 and then kept constant. In addition, the emission of AuNCs@GSH in the assembly of CDs-AuNCs was stronger than that of AuNCs@GSH alone from pH = 3.0–9.0, especially, at middle part it was increased maximally being attributed to the FRET efficiency. As it has been already demonstrated the driving force of the assembly was electrostatic interaction, with pH increasing and decreasing the deprotonation of amino group in CDs and protonation of carboxyl groups in GSH largely decreased the interaction between CDs and AuNCs, respectively, which finally induced the FRET efficiency decreased significantly. Therefore, it was suggested that both the protonated amino group in CDs and deprotonated carboxyl groups in GSH at the interface of AuNCs were vital for the assembly of CDs and AuNCs. Additionally, the results demonstrated the assembly was indeed driven by electrostatic interaction as the pH sensitivity.

To further observe the morphologies and size distributions of the assembly of CDs and AuNCs@GSH, TEM images were then employed. As shown in Fig. S7, it exhibited clearly that a big nanoparticle was displayed as a center in surrounding several smaller nanoclusters as shell. Based on the statistics over 300 individual particles, the sizes of the assembly were distributed broadly between 1.0–8.5 nm with two separated groups, centering on ∼1.65 and 5.60 nm, respectively. Being similar as the TEM results in above (Figs. 1C and 2C), the mean size of AuNCs was about 1.35 nm and that of CDs was about 4.25 nm. In addition, the lattice number of the big nanoparticle was about 0.21 nm (inset of Fig. S7), being consistent well with that of CDs. Therefore, the big nanoparticles in Fig. S7 were assigned to the CDs and the smaller ones were belonged to the AuNCs. Taken all together, it indicated that the CDs and AuNCs were successfully prepared and assembled together, where the AuNCs were adsorbed around the surface of CDs (Scheme 1 ) and therefore induced FRET from CDs to AuNCs.

In the present work a non-covalent strategy was employed to establish a FRET assembly based on electrostatic interaction, which could be regulated by the ratio of CDs to AuNCs. As it was really difficult to calculate the exact molar concentration of CDs and AuNCs in solution, all the concentrations of them were exhibited by mass to volume (mg/mL) as follows. As we all know, the transfer efficiency was a critical parameter to evaluate a FRET system, meanwhile, the lifetime was either important for a fluorescent material to distinguish different fluorophores and the microenvironment changes. Therefore, the lifetime was used to calculate the transfer efficiency in FRET as follows:
η
F R E T
= 1 −
τ
D A
τ D

Where τ DA means the lifetime of donor in the presence of the acceptor; τ D means the lifetime of the donor without the acceptor [1,44]. The QY of the assembly of CDs and AuNCs were accurately calculated by the photon counts absorbed and emitted by using fluorescence spectrophotometer with an integrating sphere. As a result, the CDs exhibited a QY of 31.95% and the AuNCs showed a value of 3.07%, respectively. Particularly, the QY of AuNCs was increased from 3.07% to 5.45% after assembly, illuminating the contribution from CDs. Finally, the transfer efficiency was calculated to be 65.7% based on the above equation, which was efficient enough for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of cysteine by using CDs-AuNCs assembly
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, the response time of CDs-AuNCs to cysteine was optimized through monitoring the fluorescence emission. The results showed the quenching response was completed within 30 min (Fig. S9). Therefore, to ensure an accurate and stable result the fluorescence determination was routinely measured at 60 min after mixing AAs with CDs-AuNCs in HEPES-NaOH buffer solution (pH = 7.4). In following, the selectivity was studied by gradual addition of each other AAs to the solution containing CDs-AuNCs assembly. However, no much change was observed for the assembly to each case in the presence of other AAs (100 μM) except cysteine at the same concentration. Therefore, a specific selection of CDs-AuNCs assembly on cysteine from other AAs was achieved in the present study (Fig. 5 ). Finally, to test the practical application of CDs-AuNCs assembly to cysteine detection, anti-interference experiment was then conducted by mixing cysteine and CDs-AuNCs in the presence of 1 equivalent each other AA. The results showed there was almost no interference on cysteine detection in the presence of other AAs (100 μM, Fig. S10). Therefore, the CDs-AuNCs assembly could be used to detect cysteine with high selectivity, sensitivity and strong anti-interference. The proposed sensing strategy was supplied a convenient assay for the related disease diagnose including heart disease, sepsis, HIV and so on, which showed great potential on the practical applications in biotechnology and life science.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of CDs-AuNCs assembly to cysteine
ce:section.ce:para: The response of CDs-AuNCs assembly to cysteine was then monitored through employing UV–vis absorption spectrum. As shown in Fig. S11A, the absorption peak at ∼420 nm for the assembly decreased gradually and finally disappeared in the presence of large amount of cysteine, indicating the surface electron energy of it was largely changed in the process. Meanwhile, a similar absorption peak was observed in the spectrum of individual AuNCs@GSH, while no absorption was shown at this position for that of CDs (Fig. S11B). Therefore, the characteristic absorption peak at ∼420 nm could be attributed to the AuNCs in the assembly. In addition, it has been reported that the free thiol group could etch the gold core to form an Au–thiol coordination complex and quench the emission of AuNCs [45,46]. Therefore, the disappeared absorption peak at ∼420 nm suggested the core of AuNCs was etched by cysteine, which induced significant emission quenching of the assembly at 605 nm. Then, glutathione (GSH) was used as a model to verify the hypothesis. The presence of it indeed induced a gradual emission quenching of the CDs-AuNCs assembly similarly (Fig. S12), confirming the free thiol group played a vital role in the quenching response. That was, the present quenching response of the assembly to cysteine was attributed to the etching to gold core.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of cysteine in fetal calf serum
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the practical application of the proposed method in clinical diagnosis, then the CDs-AuNCs assembly was used to detect cysteine in fetal calf serum, which was prepared by adding fixed amount of CDs-AuNCs into a diluted fetal calf serum (1% in HEPES-NaOH buffer solution, pH = 7.4). The gradual addition of cysteine to the system induced obvious emission quenching of AuNCs (Fig. S13), and a linear response to low concentration of cysteine (≤60 μM) was obtained. Thus, the proposed method was successfully used to detect trace amount of cysteine in serum, with significant implications for the clinical potential of this approach. In addition, three different amounts of cysteine was added to the diluted fetal calf serum, respectively, to get a final concentration between 4.0 and 50 μM (Nos. as #1–3) and then each was mixed with CDs-AuNCs assembly. The amounts of the involved cysteine could be quantitatively determined basing on the standard curve equation supplied by the proposed method. The test results shown in Table S3 indicated the recovery rates were from 97.0% to 102.6% being satisfactory for the practical purpose. Therefore, the proposed method was feasible for cysteine detection in practical applications.

A FRET assembly of CDs and AuNCs was constructed successfully by using non-covalent assembly method based on supramolecular interactions, which exhibited high transfer efficiency (65.7%) being sufficient enough for practical applications. In the assembly, the CDs performed as donor and AuNCs displayed as acceptor, which emitted at 465 and 605 nm, respectively. And the results indicated that the AuNCs were adsorbed on the surface of CDs in assembly, especially, the assembly was enhanced the QY of AuNCs from 3.07% to 5.35%, which provided a new method to improve the fluorescence QY of AuNCs. Finally, the FRET assembly was used as a ratiometric probe for cysteine detection, as which could etch the gold core and quench the emission of AuNCs. And the detection showed very high selectivity to cysteine among other amino acids and strong anti-interference. The detection was also very sensitive to cysteine with a limit of detection (LOD) at 100 nM, in particular, it could be used to detect cysteine in fetal calf serum showing very high potential in practical applications. Therefore, the present study has shed some light on the construction of FRET assembly with AuNCs, which was used to detect cysteine with high selectivity and sensitivity and strong anti-interference.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We greatly appreciate the financial support from the projects of NSFC (Nos. 91027027, 21373101 and 21003061), the Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province (20160101293JC) and the Innovation Program of the State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Jilin University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.02.072.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: FRET – Förster Resonance Energy Transfer: From Theory to Applications
Recent advances in FRET for the study of protein interactions and dynamics
The structure of cyanine 5 terminally attached to double-stranded DNA: implications for FRET studies
Materials for fluorescence resonance energy transfer analysis: beyond traditional donor-acceptor combinations
Size-dependent energy transfer between CdSe/ZnS quantum dots and gold nanoparticles
Recent advances in nanoparticle-based Forster resonance energy transfer for biosensing, molecular imaging and drug release profiling
Quantum dots as simultaneous acceptors and donors in time-gated forster resonance energy transfer relays: characterization and biosensing
Emerging non-traditional Forster resonance energy transfer configurations with semiconductor quantum dots: investigations and applications
Photoswitchable semiconductor nanocrystals with self-regulating photochromic Forster resonance energy transfer acceptors
Probing protein kinase (CK2) and alkaline phosphatase with CdSe/ZnS quantum dots
Evaluating the intracellular stability and unpacking of DNA nanocomplexes by quantum dots-FRET
Quantum dot DNA bioconjugates: attachment chemistry strongly influences the resulting composite architecture
Beta-cyclodextrin-modified CdSe/ZnS quantum dots for sensing and chiroselective analysis
Energy transfer sensitization of luminescent gold nanoclusters: more than just the classical forster mechanism
FRET-based small-molecule fluorescent probes: rational design and bioimaging applications
Carbon dots as photosensitisers for solar-driven catalysis
The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): current state and future perspective
Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism
Carbon and graphene quantum dots for optoelectronic and energy devices: a review
Carbon dots-Emerging light emitters for bioimaging, cancer therapy and optoelectronics
Sub-nanometre sized metal clusters: from synthetic challenges to the unique property discoveries
Synthesis, optical properties and applications of ultra-small luminescent gold nanoclusters
Mechanistic exploration and controlled synthesis of precise thiolate-gold nanoclusters
Metal nanoclusters: new fluorescent probes for sensors and bioimaging
Atomically precise gold nanoclusters as new model catalysts
Heterogeneous assembled nanocomplexes for ratiometric detection of highly reactive oxygen species in vitro and in vivo
Dumbbell-shaped carbon quantum dots/AuNCs nanohybrid as an efficient ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensing cadmium (II) ions and L-ascorbic acid
Carbon quantum dot-gold nanocluster nanosatellite for ratiometric fluorescence probe and imaging for hydrogen peroxide in living cells
Luminescent gold nanoparticles with size-Independent emission
A general route to make non-conjugated linear polymers luminescent
Identification of a highly luminescent Au-22(SG)(18) nanocluster
Toward understanding the growth mechanism: tracing all stable intermediate species from reduction of au(I)-Thiolate complexes to evolution of Au-25 nanoclusters
Fluorescence-enhanced sensing mechanism of BSA-Protected small gold-nanoclusters to Silver(I) ions in aqueous solutions
Ligand effect on the size, valence state and red/near infrared photoluminescence of bidentate thiol gold nanoclusters
Dithiothreitol-capped fluorescent gold nanoclusters: an efficient probe for detection of copper(II) ions in aqueous solution
Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-Color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging
Tunable photoluminescence across the entire visible spectrum from carbon dots excited by white light
Self-Assembled gold nanoclusters for bright fluorescence imaging and enhanced drug delivery
Photoluminescent carbon dots directly derived from polyethylene glycol and their application for cellular imaging
Carbon dot nanothermometry: intracellular photoluminescence lifetime thermal sensing
Fluorescent Au nanoclusters: recent progress and sensing applications
Understanding thiol-induced etching of luminescent gold nanoclusters

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xu Yu received his B.S. degree in Applied Chemistry and English from Yangtze University in 2016. Afterwards, he moved to Jilin University as a master student under the supervision of Professor Yuqing Wu. His research interests focus on the assembly of fluorescent gold nanoclusters for (bio) analytical applications.

Chun-Xia Zhang received her B.S. degree from Material Chemistry, Langfang Teachers University in 2017. She is currently a master candidate in State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Jilin University and her research interest is the fabrication of fluorescent functional nanoclusters for bioanalytical applications.

Lening Zhang is a lecturer in the department of thoracic surgery, China-Japan Union Hospital, Jilin University. He received his Ph.D. degree from Jilin University in 2017. Now his research interests focus on the functional bionanomaterials and biological detection.

Ya-Rong Xue studied chemistry in Xinzhou Teachers University and received her B.S. degree in 2017. She is currently a master candidate in State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Jilin University and her research interest is the fabrication of fluorescent functional nanoclusters for bioanalytical applications.

Hong-Wei Li is an associate professor in the State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Jilin University. He received his Ph.D. degree from Jilin University in 2009. His research interests include the development of fluorescent sensors, the preparation of noble metal nanoclusters and the bioapplications.

Yuqing Wu received her Master’s degree (1995) and PhD (1998) from Jilin University. She worked as a postdoc fellow in Kwansei Gakuin University of Japan in 1998. In 2000, she was approved to an associated professor, and currently, she is a professor of chemistry in Jilin University. Her current research interests are mainly focused on the molecular recognition, assembling, and the related mechanism investigation in biological, medical, and chemical systems, as well as the application and new development of various spectroscopic techniques in concern fields.
